flashcards.front: What is the main goal of parallel programming?
flashcards.back: To decrease execution time and increase computational throughput by using multiple processing units to work on different parts of a problem simultaneously.

Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data -- multiple processors execute different instruction streams on different data streams independently. It is the most common parallel architecture (e.g., multi-core CPUs, clusters).

flashcards.front: What is the key difference between UMA and NUMA?
flashcards.back: UMA (Uniform Memory Access): memory access time is equal for all processors. NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access): access time depends on the memory location relative to the processor -- local memory is faster than remote.

flashcards.front: What is the cache coherence problem?
flashcards.back: When multiple caches hold copies of the same data and one processor updates its copy, the other copies become stale and inconsistent. Protocols like MESI solve this by invalidating or updating other caches.

flashcards.front: Name one advantage and one disadvantage of a distributed-memory system.
flashcards.back: Advantage: Highly scalable to thousands of cores without bus bottleneck. Disadvantage: More complex to program due to explicit message passing (MPI) and manual data decomposition.

Speedup is limited by the sequential (non-parallelizable) fraction of a program. Speedup = 1 / (s + (1-s)/p), where s is the serial fraction and p is the number of processors. Even with infinite processors, speedup is bounded by 1/s.

A bug that occurs when the program's correctness depends on the relative timing of two or more threads. It happens when threads access shared data without proper synchronization, leading to unpredictable results.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between data parallelism and task parallelism?
flashcards.back: Data parallelism: same operation applied to different data elements (e.g., processing different pixels). Task parallelism: different operations performed concurrently (e.g., one thread reads, another computes, another writes).

flashcards.front: What is load balancing in parallel computing?
flashcards.back: Distributing work evenly across all processors so that no processor is idle while others are still working. Poor load balancing wastes parallel resources and reduces speedup.

flashcards.front: Why did the shift from single-core to multi-core processors happen?
flashcards.back: Power consumption and heat dissipation limits (the 'power wall') made it impractical to keep increasing single-core clock speeds. Multi-core provides more performance within the same power budget by running multiple cores at lower frequencies.
